name: Psychology
description: Psychology's major figures, schools of thought, and landmark experiments

questions.q: Who established the first psychological laboratory in Leipzig in 1879, launching experimental psychology?

source.notes: Considered the 'father of experimental psychology'.

questions.q: Which Russian physiologist is famous for his experiments on classical conditioning using dogs?

source.notes: Won the 1904 Nobel Prize — for digestive physiology.

questions.q: Who developed the five-level hierarchy of human needs, topped by self-actualization?

source.notes: Introduced in his 1943 paper 'A Theory of Human Motivation.'

questions.q: Which Viennese neurologist founded psychoanalysis and the theory of the id, ego, and superego?

source.notes: The structural model was introduced in 'The Ego and the Id' (1923).

questions.q: Which projective test involves interpreting symmetric inkblots?

source.notes: Developed by Swiss psychiatrist Hermann Rorschach in 1921.

questions.q: Which school of psychology was founded by John B. Watson and further developed by B.F. Skinner?

source.notes: Focused on observable behavior, not internal mental states.

questions.q: Which 1905 intelligence test was among the first widely used IQ tests?

source.notes: Developed in France by Alfred Binet and Théodore Simon.

questions.q: Who wrote the 1890 foundational text The Principles of Psychology?

source.notes: Introduced the 'stream of consciousness' metaphor.

questions.q: Which Swiss psychologist theorized four stages of cognitive development in children?

source.notes: Sensorimotor, preoperational, concrete operational, and formal operational.

questions.q: B.F. Skinner is associated with which type of conditioning, focused on rewards and punishments?

source.notes: Skinner invented the operant conditioning chamber (Skinner box).

questions.q: Albert Bandura's famous 1961 study of children's aggression involved what toy?

source.notes: An inflatable punch-down clown doll.

questions.q: Whose 1961 Yale experiment tested obedience to authority with apparent electric shocks?

source.notes: Participants would deliver 'shocks' up to 450V to actors they couldn't see.

questions.q: Which 1971 Stanford study, halted early, simulated a prison with student participants?

source.notes: Led by Philip Zimbardo; later reassessments have questioned its scientific validity.

questions.q: Which neo-Freudian psychoanalyst introduced the collective unconscious and archetypes?

source.notes: Founder of analytical psychology.

questions.q: Which 1920 John B. Watson experiment demonstrated classical conditioning of fear in an infant?

source.notes: Ethically troubled study considered unethical by modern standards.

questions.q: What term did Leon Festinger coin to describe the discomfort of holding two conflicting beliefs?

source.notes: Published 'A Theory of Cognitive Dissonance' in 1957.

questions.q: Which psychologist developed an eight-stage theory of psychosocial development across the lifespan?

source.notes: Includes stages like 'identity vs. role confusion' and 'integrity vs. despair.'

questions.q: Which developmental theorist proposed six stages of moral reasoning, extending Piaget's work?

source.notes: Grouped into three levels: preconventional, conventional, postconventional.

questions.q: Mamie Phipps Clark's 'doll studies' research contributed to the arguments in which landmark 1954 U.S. Supreme Court decision?

source.notes: The first Black woman to earn a psychology PhD from Columbia.

questions.q: Which psychologist founded positive psychology in 1998 after earlier developing 'learned helplessness'?

source.notes: Served as APA president in 1998.

questions.q: Which Soviet psychologist developed the 'zone of proximal development' concept in child learning?

source.notes: Died of tuberculosis in 1934 at age 37.

questions.q: Mary Ainsworth developed which experimental procedure to classify infant attachment styles?

source.notes: Identified secure, insecure-avoidant, and insecure-resistant patterns.
